content: What is the agentic loop?

The **agentic loop** is the core iterative process that AI agents use to accomplish tasks. It typically follows this cycle:

## The Loop

1. **Perceive/Observation** — The agent gathers information about its environment, current state, or task context (e.g., reading files, checking outputs, receiving user input)

2. **Think/Reasoning** — The agent processes the information, plans next steps, and decides what action to take (e.g., "I need to read this file first," or "I should search for more information")

3. **Act/Action** — The agent executes an action using available tools (e.g., calling APIs, running commands, writing files, sending messages)

4. **Repeat** — The agent observes the results of its action and continues the loop until the goal is achieved

## Why It Matters

This loop enables agents to:
- Work autonomously toward complex goals
- Adapt based on feedback from actions
- Break down multi-step tasks
- Self-correct when things don't go as planned

It's sometimes called the **observe-think-act** loop or **perceive-reason-act** cycle. This is fundamentally what distinguishes an *agent* from a simple chatbot—agents can take iterative actions in the world rather than just producing a single response.

This is sometimes called the "observe-think-act" loop or "perceive-reason-act" loop. It's fundamental to how autonomous agents operate, allowing them to iteratively work toward goals rather than just producing a single response.

1| # Lab 8 — The Agent is the Interface
2| 
3| [Sync your fork](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/working-with-forks/syncing-a-fork#syncing-a-fork-branch-from-the-command-line) regularly — the lab gets updated.
4| 
5| ## Product brief
6| 
7| > Your team has been running the LMS backend for weeks. Everyone queries data through the React dashboard or Swagger UI. Your team lead wants a new kind of interface: an AI agent that anyone can talk to in natural language. Nanobot is a lighter version of OpenClaw, and this kind of agent is becoming the new intelligent AI user interface. Instead of clicking through dashboards, users just ask questions — "which lab has the lowest pass rate?", "any errors in the last hour?" — and the agent figures out which API calls to make.
8| >
9| > Set it up from scratch. Wire it into the system. Then extend it with observability tools so it can answer questions about system health too.
10| 
11| > [!IMPORTANT]
12| > Do the whole lab on your **VM**. You can work through a plain SSH shell or through `VS Code` Remote-SSH. When this guide says `localhost`, it means the VM itself or a forwarded port from that VM. Do not install or run `nanobot` on your main machine.
13| 
14| ## What you will learn
15| 
16| By the end of this lab, you should be able to say:
17| 
18| > 1. I can explain what makes an AI agent different from a regular client like a web app or a bot.
19| >    It is not just a self-hosted chat window: it has tools, skills, memory, and can act proactively.
20| > 2. I set up nanobot from scratch — created the project, installed the framework, connected it to the Qwen API, wired it into Docker Compose, and talked to it.
21| > 3. I saw what a bare agent does without tools (hallucinates) vs. with MCP tools (answers correctly) — and I understand why.
22| > 4. I built MCP tools that let the agent query logs and traces, turning observability data into a conversational interface.
23| > 5. I used the agent to investigate a failure, fix a planted bug, and configure it to report system health proactively.
24| 
25| ## Architecture
26| 
27| By the end of the lab, the system looks like this.
28| 
29| In Lab 7 you built one client around your own LLM loop. Here, the agent becomes a shared system layer that multiple clients can talk to — and that layer has reusable tools, memory, and scheduled actions.
30| 
31| ```
32| [Browser]            [Telegram, optional]
33|     \                       /
34|      \                     /
35|       +---- [Nanobot Agent] ---- [LLM]
36|                  |
37|          +-------+-------+
38|          |               |
39|    [LMS Tools]   [Observability Tools]
40|          |               |
41|    [LMS Backend]    [Logs / Traces]
42|          |
43|     [Postgres]
44| ```
45| 
46| ### What you start with
47| 
48| - **LMS app**: the React dashboard, FastAPI backend, and PostgreSQL database.
49| - **Platform services**: Caddy reverse-proxies all traffic, and the Qwen Code API gives your agent access to the LLM.
50| - **Observability stack**: OpenTelemetry Collector, VictoriaLogs, and VictoriaTraces already collect system telemetry.
51| 
52| ### What you add
53| 
54| - **Nanobot agent**: a natural-language interface to the LMS that can reason, call tools, and answer questions.
55| - **Web chat client**: a WebSocket channel plus a Flutter web UI at `/flutter`, protected by `NANOBOT_ACCESS_KEY`.
56| - **New agent capabilities**: LMS MCP tools first, then observability MCP tools, then a scheduled health-check job.
57|

69| 2. [Deploy and Connect a Web Client](./lab/tasks/required/task-2.md) — Dockerize nanobot, add WebSocket channel + Flutter chat UI

71| 4. [Diagnose a Failure and Make the Agent Proactive](./lab/tasks/required/task-4.md) — investigate a failure, schedule in-chat health checks, fix a planted bug

### What You'll Build
An AI agent (nanobot) that serves as a natural-language interface to the LMS system, with:
- **LMS MCP tools** — query the backend, answer questions about labs/pass rates/errors

```
[Browser/Telegram] → [Nanobot Agent] → [LLM (Qwen)]

8| This directory contains architecture and requirements documentation for the **Learning Management Service (LMS)** — the system students work with in the Software Engineering Toolkit course.
9| 
10| The LMS is grounded in the **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender) [^OBER] data model, which extends the classic user–item schema with learning outcomes and assessment items.
11| 
12| ## Structure
13| 
14| ```tree
15| docs/
16| ├── README.md                    # This file
17| ├── design/
18| │   └── architecture.md          # C4 architecture: context, containers, components, flows
19| └── requirements/
20|     └── vision.md                # Project vision, features, and actors
21| ```
22| 
23| ## Getting Started
24| 
25| 1. Start with [requirements/vision.md](requirements/vision.md) to understand what the system does and who uses it.
26| 2. Then read [design/architecture.md](design/architecture.md) for how it is built and how the components interact.
27| 
28| To view Mermaid diagrams, open the files in a Markdown viewer with Mermaid support (e.g., VS Code with the Markdown Preview Mermaid Support extension, or GitHub).
29| 
30| ## References
31| 
32| [^OBER]: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18186>

32| ---
33| 
34| ## 1. Introduction
35| 
36| This document describes the architecture of the **Learning Management Service (LMS)** — the system students deploy, test, and extend in the Software Engineering Toolkit labs.
37| 
38| The architecture follows the **C4 model**, progressing from a high-level system context (what it is and who uses it) down to containers (how it is deployed), components (how the code is structured), and interaction flows (how the pieces work together at runtime).
39| 
40| The data model is grounded in **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender): items can *promote* or *verify* learning outcomes, and learner interactions are logged so that outcome mastery can be calculated from those logs.
41| 
42| ---
43| 
44| ## 2. Context Diagram
45| 
46| The context diagram shows the system boundary and the external actors that interact with it.
47| 
48| ```mermaid
49| graph TD
50|     subgraph "Learning Management System"
51|         LMS[Learning Management Service]
52|     end
53| 
54|     subgraph "External Actors"
55|         Student[Student / Learner]
56|         Admin[Admin / Instructor]
57|         Developer[Developer]
58|     end
59| 
60|     Student <-->|"Browse items\nLog interactions"| LMS
61|     Admin <-->|"Inspect data\nRun SQL queries"| LMS
62|     Developer <-->|"Test and extend\nthe API"| LMS
63| ```
64| 
65| ### External Actors
66| 
67| | Actor              | Description                                                                                   |
68| | ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
69| | Student / Learner  | Uses the React frontend to browse learning items; interaction events are logged automatically |
70| | Admin / Instructor | Manages the database via pgAdmin; validates API responses via Swagger UI                      |
71| | Developer          | Writes unit and end-to-end tests; extends the system as part of lab exercises                 |
72| 
73| ---
74| 
75| ## 3. Domain Model
76| 
77| The domain model is based on OBER: it extends the classic *Learner–Item* schema with a hierarchy for learning content and a log of interactions.
78| 
79| ```mermaid
80| classDiagram
81|     class Item {
82|         +int id
83|         +str type
84|         +int parent_id
85|         +str title
86|         +str description
87|         +JSONB attributes
88|         +datetime created_at
89|     }
90| 
91|     class Learner {
92|         +int id
93|         +str name
94|         +str email
95|         +datetime enrolled_at
96|     }
97| 
98|     class Interacts {
99|         +int id
100|         +int learner_id
101|         +int item_id
102|         +str kind
103|         +datetime created_at
104|     }
105| 
106|     Item "0..1" --> "0..*" Item : parent_id (tree)
107|     Learner "1" --> "0..*" Interacts : logs
108|     Item "1" --> "0..*" Interacts : tracked in
109| ```
110| 
111| ### Entity Descriptions
112| 
113| | Entity      | Description                                                                                                                                                                                   |
114| | ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
115| | `Item`      | Any piece of learning content. Forms a tree via `parent_id`. `type` is one of: `course`, `lab`, `task`, `step`. `attributes` holds type-specific metadata (e.g. instructors, dates) as JSONB. |
116| | `Learner`   | A student enrolled in the system. Identified by email (unique).                                                                                                                               |
117| | `Interacts` | One interaction event: a learner engaging with an item. `kind` is one of: `view`, `attempt`, `complete`.                                                                                      |
118| 
119| ### Key Relationships
120| 
121| - **Item tree**: Items reference a `parent_id` forming a four-level hierarchy — `course → lab → task → step`. Root items have `parent_id = NULL`.
122| - **Interaction log**: Every `Interacts` record links one `Learner` to one `Item` with a `kind` (what happened) and a timestamp (when).
123| - **OBER extension point**: Items carry a `type` and `attributes` that can express whether an item *promotes* (teaches) or *verifies* (assesses) a learning outcome — enabling mastery calculation from the interaction log.
124| 
125| ---
126| 
127| ## 4. Container Diagram
128| 
129| The system is deployed as seven Docker containers, orchestrated by Docker Compose. The React and Flutter frontends are compiled into static files and served by Caddy. The nanobot AI agent exposes a WebSocket endpoint that both the Flutter web app and the Telegram bot connect to.
130| 
131| ```mermaid
132| graph TD
133|     Student([Student / Learner])
134|     Admin([Admin / Instructor])
135|     Developer([Developer])
136| 
137|     subgraph "Learning Management System — Docker Compose"
138|         CADDY["Caddy\n[Reverse Proxy + Static Server]\nServes frontends, proxies API\nHost port :42002"]
139|         API["FastAPI\n[Python, SQLModel, Uvicorn]\nREST API with Swagger UI\nContainer port :8000\nHost port :42001"]
140|         DB[("PostgreSQL\n[Relational Database]\nStores items, learners,\nand interactions\nHost port :42004")]
141|         PGA["pgAdmin\n[Web UI]\nDatabase management\nHost port :42003"]
142|         NANOBOT["Nanobot\n[Python, nanobot-ai]\nAI agent gateway\nWebSocket :8765\nHTTP :18790"]
143|         TGBOT["Telegram Bot\n[Python, aiogram]\nWebSocket relay\nto Nanobot"]
144|     end
145| 
146|     Student -->|"Opens app (browser)\nHTTP :42002"| CADDY
147|     Developer -->|"Swagger UI"| CADDY
148|     Student -->|"Telegram"| TGBOT
149|     CADDY -->|"Serves static files\n(React SPA, Flutter)"| Student
150|     CADDY -->|"Reverse proxy\nAPI requests"| API
151|     CADDY -->|"WebSocket\n/ws/chat"| NANOBOT
152|     API -->|"Async SQL\n(SQLAlchemy)"| DB
153|     NANOBOT -->|"MCP tools\n(mcp_lms_*)"| API
154|     TGBOT -->|"WebSocket\nws://nanobot:8765"| NANOBOT
155|     Admin -->|"HTTP :42003"| PGA
156|     PGA -->|SQL| DB
157| ```
158| 
159| ### Container Responsibilities
160| 
161| | Container    | Technology                         | Responsibility                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
162| | ------------ | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
163| | Caddy        | Go, Caddyfile, Node (build stage)  | Serves the React frontend as static files at `/` and reverse-proxies API paths (`/items`, `/learners`, `/interactions`, `/docs`) to FastAPI. Built via a multi-stage Dockerfile that compiles the TypeScript frontend and bundles it into the Caddy image. |
164| | FastAPI      | Python, FastAPI, SQLModel, Uvicorn | REST API: handles all business logic, validates Bearer token on every request, and exposes auto-generated Swagger UI at `/docs`.                                                                                                                           |
165| | PostgreSQL   | PostgreSQL                         | Relational database: stores the `item`, `learner`, and `interacts` tables. Initialised with schema and seed data from `init.sql` on first startup.                                                                                                         |
166| | pgAdmin      | pgAdmin 4                          | Web-based database management UI: lets admins inspect tables, run SQL queries, and browse the data.                                                                                                                                                        |
167| | Nanobot      | Python, nanobot-ai                 | AI agent gateway: accepts WebSocket connections on port 8765, routes messages to an LLM agent that can query the LMS backend via MCP tools. See [nanobot.md](../../wiki/nanobot.md) for internals.                                                         |
168| | Telegram Bot | Python, aiogram                    | Standalone Telegram bot: handles `/start` and `/help` locally; forwards all other messages to nanobot over WebSocket. Per-user API key auth via `/login`.                                                                                                  |
169| |              |                                    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
170| 
171| ---
172| 
173| ## 5. Component Diagram
174| 
175| ### 5.1 FastAPI Application
176| 
177| The FastAPI application is structured into four layers: HTTP routers, middleware, database access, and data models.
178| 
179| ```mermaid
180| graph TD
181|     subgraph "FastAPI Application"
182|         subgraph "HTTP Routers"
183|             IR["Items Router\nGET /items\nGET /items/{id}\nPOST /items\nPUT /items/{id}"]
184|             INTR["Interactions Router\nGET /interactions\nPOST /interactions"]
185|             LR["Learners Router\nGET /learners\nPOST /learners"]
186|         end
187| 
188|         subgraph "Middleware"
189|             AUTH["Auth Middleware\nHTTPBearer\nverify_api_key()"]
190|         end
191| 
192|         subgraph "Database Access"
193|             IDB["items.py\nCRUD: get, create, update"]
194|             INTDB["interactions.py\nQuery: list, filter by item"]
195|             LDB["learners.py\nCRUD: list, create"]
196|             CONN["database.py\nAsync SQLAlchemy engine\nSession factory"]
197|         end
198| 
199|         subgraph "Models (SQLModel)"
200|             IM["Item\nItemCreate\nItemUpdate\nItemRecord"]
201|             INTM["InteractionLog\nInteractionLogCreate\nInteractionModel"]
202|             LM["Learner\nLearnerCreate"]
203|         end
204| 
205|         CFG["settings.py\nPydantic Settings\n(env vars)"]
206|     end
207| 
208|     IR --> AUTH
209|     INTR --> AUTH
210|     LR --> AUTH
211|     IR --> IDB
212|     INTR --> INTDB
213|     LR --> LDB
214|     IDB --> CONN
215|     INTDB --> CONN
216|     LDB --> CONN
217|     IDB --> IM
218|     INTDB --> INTM
219|     LDB --> LM
220|     AUTH --> CFG
221| ```
222| 
223| #### Component Descriptions
224| 
225| | Component           | File                      | Description                                                                                                |
226| | ------------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
227| | Items Router        | `routers/items.py`        | CRUD endpoints for learning items. Always enabled.                                                         |
228| | Interactions Router | `routers/interactions.py` | Read and create endpoints for interaction logs. Enabled via `BACKEND_ENABLE_INTERACTIONS=true`.            |
229| | Learners Router     | `routers/learners.py`     | CRUD endpoints for learner profiles. Enabled via `BACKEND_ENABLE_LEARNERS=true`.                           |
230| | Auth Middleware     | `auth.py`                 | Validates the `Authorization: Bearer <key>` header on every request. Key configured via `API_KEY` env var. |
231| | Items DB            | `db/items.py`             | Async database operations for the `item` table.                                                            |
232| | Interactions DB     | `db/interactions.py`      | Async database operations for the `interacts` table.                                                       |
233| | Learners DB         | `db/learners.py`          | Async database operations for the `learner` table.                                                         |
234| | Database Connection | `database.py`             | Creates and manages the async SQLAlchemy engine and session factory.                                       |
235| | Models              | `models/`                 | SQLModel classes: define table schema, validate input (Pydantic), and shape API responses.                 |
236| | Settings            | `settings.py`             | Pydantic `BaseSettings`: reads all configuration from environment variables.                               |
237| 
238| ---
239| 
240| ## 6. Sequence Diagrams
241| 
242| ### 6.1 Student Fetches Items via Frontend
243| 
244| The most common interaction: a student opens the browser, Caddy serves the React SPA as static files, and the SPA calls the API through Caddy. The API key is entered at runtime through the UI and persisted in `localStorage`.
245| 
246| ```mermaid
247| sequenceDiagram
248|     actor Student
249|     participant Browser as React SPA
250|     participant Caddy
251|     participant API as FastAPI
252|     participant DB as PostgreSQL
253| 
254|     Student->>Caddy: GET / (opens app in browser)
255|     Caddy-->>Browser: index.html + JS bundle (static files)
256|     Note over Student,Browser: Student enters API key in the UI
257|     Browser->>Browser: Save the key to localStorage
258|     Browser->>Caddy: GET /items (Authorization: Bearer <token>)
259|     Caddy->>API: Proxy GET /items
260|     API->>API: verify_api_key()
261|     API->>DB: SELECT * FROM item ORDER BY id
262|     DB-->>API: list of item rows
263|     API-->>Caddy: 200 OK — JSON [{id, type, title, ...}]
264|     Caddy-->>Browser: 200 OK — JSON [{id, type, title, ...}]
265|     Note over Browser: Renders items table
266| ```
... (truncated)

1| # Learning Management Service — Vision
2| 
3| ## 1. Introduction
4| 
5| The Learning Management Service (LMS) is a backend API system built for the Software Engineering Toolkit course at Innopolis University. It is grounded in the **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender) framework, which places learning outcomes at the center of the system — enabling evaluation of learning trajectories based on actual mastery rather than engagement metrics alone.
6| 
7| Students interact with the LMS throughout the course labs: they deploy it, test it, extend it with a front-end, and use it as the subject of software engineering exercises.
8| 
9| ## 2. Business Context
10| 
11| - Learning content is organized as a hierarchy: courses contain labs, labs contain tasks, tasks contain steps.
12| - Learner interactions (views, attempts, completions) are recorded against items to track progress.
13| - The data schema follows OBER: items can *promote* or *verify* learning outcomes, and mastery is calculated from interaction logs.
14| - The system serves as the hands-on subject of lab exercises in testing, deployment, front-end development, and AI-assisted coding.
15| 
16| ## 3. Vision of the Solution
17| 
18| ### 3.1 Vision Statement
19| 
20| > Provide a deployable, testable REST API that models learning content hierarchically, records learner interactions, and lays the foundation for outcome-based assessment — serving as the practical subject of the Software Engineering Toolkit lab exercises.
21| 
22| ### 3.2 Main Features
23| 
24| | ID  | Feature                  | Description                                                               |
25| | --- | ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
26| | F1  | Learning item tree       | Store items in a four-level hierarchy: course → lab → task → step         |
27| | F2  | Item CRUD                | Create, read, and update items via REST endpoints                         |
28| | F3  | Interaction logging      | Record learner interactions (view, attempt, complete) against items       |
29| | F4  | Learner management       | Manage learner profiles: name, email, enrollment date                     |
30| | F5  | Bearer token auth        | Authenticate all API requests with a configurable Bearer token            |
31| | F6  | Swagger UI               | Auto-generated interactive API documentation at `/docs`                   |
32| | F7  | React front-end          | Browser SPA that fetches and displays the item list                       |
33| | F8  | Reverse proxy            | Caddy routes external HTTP traffic to the API                             |
34| | F9  | Database admin UI        | pgAdmin provides a web interface for inspecting and querying the database |
35| | F10 | Containerised deployment | Docker Compose orchestrates all services for local and VM deployment      |
36| 
37| ## 4. Scope
38| 
39| **In scope:**
40| 
41| - REST API for items, interactions, and learners
42| - PostgreSQL data store with seed data
43| - React frontend displaying the item list
44| - Caddy reverse proxy routing external traffic to the API
45| - pgAdmin for database management
46| - Docker Compose orchestration
47| 
48| **Out of scope (data model supports it; not yet implemented):**
49| 
50| - Outcome mastery calculation and recommendation engine
51| - Learner self-registration and authentication
52| - Outcome tree management via API
53| 
54| ## 5. System Context
55| 
56| ```mermaid
57| graph TD
58|     subgraph "Learning Management System"
59|         LMS[Learning Management Service]
60|     end
61| 
62|     subgraph "External Actors"
63|         Student[Student / Learner]
64|         Admin[Admin / Instructor]
65|         Developer[Developer]
66|     end
67| 
68|     Student <-->|Browse items, log interactions| LMS
69|     Admin <-->|Manage and inspect data| LMS
70|     Developer <-->|Test and extend the API| LMS
71| ```
72| 
73| ## 6. External Actors
74| 
75| | Actor              | Description                                                                        |
76| | ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
77| | Student / Learner  | Uses the frontend to browse learning items and logs interactions as they study     |
78| | Admin / Instructor | Inspects the database via pgAdmin; validates data via Swagger UI                   |
79| | Developer          | Writes and runs tests against the API; extends the system as part of lab exercises |
80| 
81| ## 7. Quality Attributes
82| 
83| ### Deployability
84| 
85| The system must be startable with a single command (`docker compose up`) on a fresh Linux VM without manual configuration beyond setting environment variables.
86| 
87| ### Testability
88| 
89| The API must be fully testable via unit tests (business logic in isolation) and end-to-end tests (HTTP requests against a running instance). Intentional bugs are present for students to discover through testing.
90| 
91| ### Maintainability
92| 
93| The codebase must be readable and modifiable by students with basic Python and TypeScript knowledge, following familiar patterns (FastAPI routers, SQLModel, React functional components).
94| 
95| ### Security
96| 
97| All API endpoints must be protected with Bearer token authentication. No endpoint is accessible without a valid token.

(End of file — 97 lines total)

# LMS System Architecture

## Overview

The **Learning Management Service (LMS)** is a containerized educational platform built for the Software Engineering Toolkit course. It follows the **OBER** (Outcome-Based Educational Recommender) data model and uses a **monolithic layered architecture** with Docker Compose orchestration.

---

## 🏗️ High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        External Actors                                   │
│    Student/Browser    Admin/pgAdmin    Developer/Swagger    Telegram    │
└────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬─────────────┬────────┘
             │                  │                  │             │
             ▼                  ▼                  ▼             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                           Caddy Reverse Proxy                           │
│                    (Port 42002 - Single Entry Point)                    │
│   Routes: / → React, /flutter → Flutter, /api/* → Backend, /ws → Nano   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             │                  │                  │             │
             ▼                  ▼                  ▼             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Application Layer                                │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  │
│  │ React SPA    │  │ Flutter App  │  │ FastAPI      │  │ Nanobot    │  │
│  │ (static)     │  │ (static)     │  │ Backend      │  │ AI Agent   │  │
│  │              │  │              │  │ :8000        │  │ :8765 WS   │  │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └─────┬──────┘  │
│                                              │                │         │
│                                              ▼                ▼         │
│                                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐    │
│                                    │   MCP Tools (LMS + Obs)      │    │
│                                    └──────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             │                  │                  │
             ▼                  ▼                  ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Data & Observability Layer                       │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  │
│  │ PostgreSQL   │  │ VictoriaLogs │  │ Victoria     │  │ OTel       │  │
│  │ Database     │  │ (Logs)       │  │ Traces       │  │ Collector  │  │
│  │ :42004       │  │              │  │              │  │            │  │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 📦 Container Services

| Service | Technology | Port | Responsibility |
|---------|------------|------|----------------|
| **Caddy** | Go | 42002 | Reverse proxy + static file server (React, Flutter) |
| **FastAPI** | Python, SQLModel, Uvicorn | 42001 | REST API with Swagger UI at `/docs` |
| **PostgreSQL** | PostgreSQL 18 | 42004 | Relational database (items, learners, interactions) |
| **pgAdmin** | pgAdmin 4 | 42003 | Web-based database management UI |
| **Nanobot** | Python, nanobot-ai | 8765 (WS), 18790 (HTTP) | AI agent gateway with MCP tools |
| **Qwen Code API** | Python proxy | 8080 | LLM API gateway to Qwen |
| **VictoriaLogs** | VictoriaMetrics | - | Log storage (7-day retention) |
| **VictoriaTraces** | VictoriaMetrics | - | Distributed trace storage |
| **OTel Collector** | OpenTelemetry | - | Collects & forwards logs/traces |
| **Telegram Bot** *(optional)* | Python, aiogram | - | WebSocket relay to Nanobot |

---

## 🗄️ Data Model (OBER-Based)

```
┌─────────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────────┐
│       Item          │       │      Learner        │
├─────────────────────┤       ├─────────────────────┤
│ id (PK)             │       │ id (PK)             │
│ type                │       │ name                │
│ parent_id (FK) ─────┼──┐    │ email               │
│ title               │  │    │ enrolled_at         │
│ description         │  │    └──────────┬──────────┘
│ attributes (JSONB)  │  │               │
│ created_at          │  │               │
└─────────────────────┘  │               │
         ▲               │               │
         │               │               ▼
         │               │    ┌─────────────────────┐
         └───────────────┴───►│     Interacts       │
                              ├─────────────────────┤
                              │ id (PK)             │
                              │ learner_id (FK)     │
                              │ item_id (FK)        │
                              │ kind (view/attempt) │
                              │ created_at          │
                              └─────────────────────┘
```

### Item Hierarchy (4 levels via `parent_id`):
```
Course → Lab → Task → Step
```

---

## 🔌 API Structure (FastAPI)

```
backend/app/
├── routers/
│   ├── items.py          # GET/POST/PUT /items
│   ├── learners.py       # GET/POST /learners (feature-flagged)
│   └── interactions.py   # GET/POST /interactions (feature-flagged)
├── db/
│   ├── items.py          # CRUD operations
│   ├── learners.py       # CRUD operations
│   └── interactions.py   # Query operations
├── models/
│   ├── item.py           # SQLModel classes
│   ├── learner.py
│   └── interaction.py
├── auth.py               # Bearer token middleware
├── database.py           # Async SQLAlchemy engine
└── settings.py           # Pydantic settings (env vars)
```

### Key Endpoints:
| Method | Endpoint | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| GET | `/items` | List all learning items |
| GET | `/items/{id}` | Get single item |
| POST | `/items` | Create new item |
| PUT | `/items/{id}` | Update item |
| GET | `/learners` | List learners |
| POST | `/learners` | Create learner |
| GET | `/interactions` | List interaction logs |
| POST | `/interactions` | Log an interaction |
| GET | `/docs` | Swagger UI |

---

## 🔐 Authentication & Security

| Layer | Mechanism |
|-------|-----------|
| **API** | `Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>` header (validated on every request) |
| **WebSocket** | Query parameter: `ws://nanobot:8765?access_key=SECRET` |
| **Database** | Internal network only; pgAdmin requires separate credentials |

---

## 🔄 Key Interaction Flows

### 1. Student Browses Items
```
Browser → Caddy (static React) → Caddy (proxy /items) → FastAPI → PostgreSQL
```

### 2. AI Agent Query
```
User → Nanobot (WebSocket) → LLM (Qwen) → MCP Tools → FastAPI → PostgreSQL
                              ↓
                        VictoriaLogs/Traces
```

### 3. Observability Pipeline
```
FastAPI → OTel Collector (gRPC) → VictoriaLogs / VictoriaTraces
```

---

## 🎯 Design Decisions

| Decision | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| **Monolith** | Easier to deploy, test, and understand for teaching |
| **Caddy** | Single-origin setup eliminates CORS; handles TLS |
| **SQLModel** | Combines SQLAlchemy + Pydantic; avoids model duplication |
| **Async DB** | Consistent with FastAPI's async model; non-blocking |
| **Feature Flags** | Control endpoint availability without code changes |
| **Standalone WebSocket** | Clean service boundary for Telegram/Flutter clients |
| **Typed Messages** | Rich UI support (buttons, confirmations) without parsing |

---

## 📁 Repository Structure

```
/root/se-toolkit-lab-8/
├── backend/              # FastAPI application
├── client-web-react/     # React frontend
├── nanobot/              # AI agent (Lab 8)
├── nanobot-websocket-channel/
│   ├── client-web-flutter/   # Flutter web client
│   └── client-telegram-bot/  # Telegram bot (optional)
├── caddy/                # Reverse proxy config
├── otel-collector/       # OpenTelemetry config
├── qwen-code-api/        # LLM API gateway
├── docs/                 # Architecture & requirements
└── docker-compose.yml    # Orchestration
```

---

This architecture supports the pedagogical goals of the course: students can deploy the entire system with `docker compose up`, explore the codebase in one sitting, and incrementally extend it across 8 labs.

content: What is the agentic loop?

The **agentic loop** is the core iterative cycle that AI agents use to accomplish tasks autonomously. It follows this pattern:

## The Cycle

1. **Perceive** — Gather information about the environment (read files, check outputs, receive user input)

2. **Think** — Process information, plan next steps, decide what action to take

3. **Act** — Execute an action using available tools (call APIs, run commands, write files)

4. **Repeat** — Observe results and continue until the goal is achieved

## Why It Matters

This **observe → think → act** loop enables agents to:
- Work autonomously toward complex goals
- Adapt based on feedback
- Break down multi-step tasks
- Self-correct when things go wrong

It's what distinguishes an *agent* from a simple chatbot—agents can take iterative actions in the world rather than just producing a single response.
